note_data.title: Further programming
keyPoints: **Object-oriented programming (OOP)** uses classes and objects to model real-world entities with attributes and methods
**Inheritance** allows a child class to inherit attributes and methods from a parent class, promoting code reuse
**Polymorphism** enables objects of different classes to be treated as objects of a common base class through method overriding
**Encapsulation** hides internal implementation details and controls access to data through private and public access modifiers
**Method overriding** occurs when a subclass provides a specific implementation of a method already defined in its parent class
**Abstract classes** cannot be instantiated and may contain abstract methods that must be implemented by subclasses
**Design patterns** provide reusable solutions to common programming problems and improve code maintainability
**Composition** involves creating objects that contain other objects as instance variables, representing 'has-a' relationships
**Constructor methods** are special methods called when an object is created to initialize the object's attributes

definitions.definition: A mechanism where a new class (subclass) inherits attributes and methods from an existing class (superclass), allowing code reuse and creating hierarchical relationships
The ability of objects of different classes to respond to the same method call in their own specific way through method overriding
The bundling of data and methods within a class while restricting direct access to internal data through access modifiers
A class that cannot be instantiated and may contain abstract methods that must be implemented by any concrete subclass
A reusable solution template for commonly occurring problems in software design that provides best practices for code structure

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
Give characteristics or features with some detail but no explanation of why
Give similarities AND differences between concepts

modelAnswer.question: Explain the difference between inheritance and composition in object-oriented programming. [4 marks]
modelAnswer.answer: Inheritance creates an 'is-a' relationship where a subclass inherits attributes and methods from a parent class, allowing code reuse through class hierarchies. Composition creates a 'has-a' relationship where an object contains other objects as instance variables. Inheritance enables polymorphism through method overriding, while composition provides greater flexibility as relationships can be changed at runtime. Inheritance creates tight coupling between classes, whereas composition promotes loose coupling and better encapsulation.
markBreakdown: Mark 1: Inheritance creates 'is-a' relationship/inherits from parent
Mark 2: Composition creates 'has-a' relationship/contains other objects

commonMistakes: Never say 'inheritance copies code' — always say 'inheritance allows access to parent class methods and attributes'
Students often confuse method overriding with method overloading — overriding redefines inherited methods, overloading creates multiple methods with same name but different parameters
Don't write 'encapsulation hides data' — say 'encapsulation controls access to data through access modifiers'
Never describe polymorphism as 'many forms' — explain it as 'objects responding to same method call differently'
Students forget that abstract classes cannot be instantiated — they can only be inherited from
examinerTips: OOP concepts appear in Paper 2 almost every year, especially inheritance and polymorphism
Always use specific programming terminology rather than general descriptions
Draw UML class diagrams to show inheritance relationships for full marks
Code examples that demonstrate method overriding score highly in explain questions
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: UML class diagram showing parent class connected to child classes with inheritance arrows, including attributes and methods with access modifiers (+ for public, - for private)
pastPaperRefs: This topic was tested in M/J 2023 P2 Q4 (6 marks) — they asked students to write a function that validates user input and returns a boolean value, requiring proper parameter passing and return statements
This topic appeared in O/N 2022 P2 Q5 (8 marks) — candidates had to create a procedure with local variables and explain the difference between local and global scope
This topic was examined in M/J 2022 P1 Q12 (2 marks) — multiple choice question testing understanding of parameter passing by value vs by reference
This topic featured in O/N 2023 P2 Q3 (5 marks) — students needed to trace through a recursive function and show the values returned at each level

workedExample.question: Write a function called FACTORIAL that takes an integer parameter N and returns the factorial of N using recursion. Show how the function would be called to calculate 4! (6 marks)
steps: Step 1: Define function header with appropriate parameter - FUNCTION FACTORIAL(N : INTEGER) RETURNS INTEGER
Step 2: Identify base case - IF N <= 1 THEN RETURN 1
Step 3: Implement recursive case - ELSE RETURN N * FACTORIAL(N-1)
Step 5: Show function call - Result ← FACTORIAL(4)
workedExample.answer:
FUNCTION FACTORIAL(N : INTEGER) RETURNS INTEGER
    IF N <= 1 THEN
        RETURN 1
    ELSE
        RETURN N * FACTORIAL(N-1)
    ENDIF
ENDFUNCTION

Result ← FACTORIAL(4)
This returns 24
workedExample.markScheme: 1 mark for correct function header, 1 mark for base case, 2 marks for recursive call, 1 mark for correct function call syntax, 1 mark for showing correct result

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates confused procedures and functions' — always remember that functions RETURN a value while procedures perform actions without returning values
Examiner Report 2022: 'Students frequently forgot to declare local variables properly' — always declare variables within functions/procedures using appropriate data types and scope
Examiner Report 2023: 'Recursive solutions often missed the base case' — every recursive function must have a clear terminating condition to prevent infinite loops
note_data.keyDiagram: Students must draw a stack diagram showing function calls and returns. Draw boxes stacked vertically, each representing a function call with its local variables and parameters. Show arrows indicating the flow of execution downward (calls) and upward (returns). Include memory allocation showing how local variables are created and destroyed. For recursion, show multiple instances of the same function with different parameter values stacked on top of each other.
note_data.quickRecap: Functions return values and use RETURN statements, procedures don't return values. Local variables exist only within their function/procedure scope. Parameters pass data into functions/procedures. Recursion requires a base case to terminate and a recursive case that calls itself with modified parameters.
